key_insight: The paper treats each attention token as a bare matrix Lie group element and scores token pairs with a closed-form Lie-algebra distance on the relative transform, making attention intrinsically equivariant without learned kernels or representation machinery.
engineering_relevance: This enables parameter-efficient, symmetry-preserving attention for geometric data on groups such as SE(2), SO(3), and even non-compact affine groups, which is useful for robotics, pose reasoning, and transformation-aware sequence models.
method: Use group tokens g_i ∈ G directly, with relative pose g_i^{-1}g_j as the canonical pairwise relation.
Map the relative pose through a logarithm chart and score by s_ij = -||log(g_i^{-1}g_j)||_λ^2 / τ.
Avoid irrep-based features and learned pairwise kernels; equivariance follows from diagonal group action.
evidence_signal: On sequence-completion tests over SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2), the closed-form score matched an MLP kernel on the same invariant, outperformed it on SE(2), used 50–80× fewer score parameters, and preserved invariance far better than a vector-token baseline.
watch_item: The method depends on a logarithm chart that must contain the relative poses, so practical use may be limited by chart singularities or by group elements outside the chosen log domain.
paper_title: The Token Is a Group Element: On Lie-Algebra Attention over Matrix Lie Groups
